content.text: Incremental Architecture Evolution for AI Systems

Architecture evolution in Claude integrations is structurally different from architecture evolution in deterministic software systems. In a deterministic system, a function that was correct last week will be correct today, so an architecture change that passes the test suite can be deployed with confidence. In a Claude integration, a prompt change that improved response quality on the golden test set may produce degraded output on real user inputs that the golden set does not cover, and a model version upgrade that passes all existing tests may change behaviour in ways that only emerge after a week of production traffic. These properties require an architecture evolution approach that is more conservative than is typical for deterministic code.

attrs.alt: Incremental architecture evolution: Stabilise → Extract → Test → Cut Over, with Never Big-Bang, Parallel Run First, and Fitness Function principles

content.text: The four-step pattern above applies to any architectural change in a Claude integration: moving from a monolithic prompt to a multi-step chain, replacing a direct API call with a wrapper service, migrating from one model version to another, or splitting a single integration into multiple specialised ones. The pattern's core principle is that no change should modify more than one architectural layer at a time: changing the prompt and the API client in the same PR makes it impossible to isolate which change caused a regression.
Fitness Functions for Claude Integrations

An architectural fitness function is an automated test that verifies a specific architectural property — not the behaviour of a specific function, but an invariant that the architecture must maintain. In Claude integrations, fitness functions verify properties like "all Claude API calls go through the retry handler", "no prompt string is assembled at the call site rather than in the prompt builder", and "response parsing is always done in a dedicated parsing function, not inline". These invariants define the architectural boundaries the team has decided to maintain.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum

class FitnessFunctionStatus(str, Enum):
    PASS    = "pass"
    FAIL    = "fail"
    WARNING = "warning"  # constraint is soft; violation is notable but not blocking

@dataclass
class ArchitecturalFitnessFunction:
    function_id: str
    name: str
    invariant: str          # human-readable statement of the invariant
    automated: bool         # True = runs in CI; False = requires manual check
    blocking: bool          # True = merge blocked on failure
    last_checked_status: FitnessFunctionStatus = FitnessFunctionStatus.WARNING

    def check_description(self) -> str:
        mode = "CI-automated" if self.automated else "manual"
        gate = "merge-blocking" if self.blocking else "advisory"
        return f"{self.function_id} ({mode}, {gate}): {self.invariant}"

# Example fitness functions for a typical Claude integration
STANDARD_FITNESS_FUNCTIONS = [
    ArchitecturalFitnessFunction(
        function_id="FF-01",
        name="Claude calls through wrapper",
        invariant="No direct anthropic.messages.create calls outside of ClaudeClient class",
        automated=True,
        blocking=True,
    ),
    ArchitecturalFitnessFunction(
        function_id="FF-02",
        name="Prompt assembly in builder",
        invariant="All prompt string construction occurs in PromptBuilder; no f-string prompt at call sites",
        automated=True,
        blocking=True,
    ),
    ArchitecturalFitnessFunction(
        function_id="FF-03",
        name="Response parsing isolated",
        invariant="Response text is passed to a parse_* function before use; no inline .split() or regex on message.content",
        automated=True,
        blocking=False,  # advisory: warns but doesn't block during transition
    ),
    ArchitecturalFitnessFunction(
        function_id="FF-04",
        name="Feedback events emitted",
        invariant="Every Claude interaction emits a FeedbackEvent; no silent interactions",
        automated=True,
        blocking=True,
    ),
]

The STANDARD_FITNESS_FUNCTIONS list defines the architectural invariants that most Claude integrations should enforce. FF-01 ensures all API calls go through the wrapper, which is where retry logic, logging, and cost tracking live. FF-02 ensures prompt strings are not assembled at call sites, which would make prompt evolution difficult — a prompt change should be a change in the PromptBuilder, not a search-and-replace across the codebase. FF-04 ensures every interaction is observable through the feedback pipeline; a Claude call that does not emit a FeedbackEvent is a blind spot in the quality measurement system.
The Strangler Fig Pattern for Prompt Architecture

The strangler fig pattern — building the new system around the old one, routing traffic gradually, and only removing the old system after the new one is fully proven — is the standard approach for large architectural migrations. For Claude integrations, the pattern applies specifically to prompt architecture migrations: moving from a single-turn prompt to a multi-turn conversation, from a single model to a routing system that chooses between models, or from a direct prompt to a tool-use architecture. Each of these migrations can be executed incrementally without taking the integration offline.

The deterministic routing via request_id hash is important for user consistency: a user whose request is routed to the new path should see the new path consistently across all their requests during the rollout period, not randomly alternating between architectures. The shadow mode flag enables a pre-rollout verification phase: the new architecture processes all requests and logs outputs without serving them to users. This allows the team to compare new vs. legacy outputs across the full request distribution before committing any user-visible traffic to the new path.
Parallel Run Logging for Architecture Comparison

During a shadow-mode or partial-rollout migration, the team has the opportunity to compare new and legacy architecture outputs on the same inputs. This comparison is the primary mechanism for verifying that the new architecture is a safe replacement — not the golden set (which covers known inputs) but the full production request distribution (which covers all inputs). The comparison log enables two forms of analysis: qualitative sampling (reading random pairs of outputs to spot systematic differences) and quantitative measurement (computing the fraction of requests where the outputs are materially different).

The migration_readiness method defines the gate for proceeding from shadow mode to live traffic. A divergence rate below 5% with an empty review queue means the new architecture produces substantially equivalent outputs to the legacy architecture on real production inputs. The 5% threshold is not a guarantee of equivalence — it means the new path differs on fewer than 1 in 20 requests, which for most integrations is within acceptable variance. Teams migrating high-stakes integrations (where incorrect responses have significant consequences) should use a tighter threshold; teams migrating low-stakes integrations can use a looser one.
Managing Model Version Evolution as an Architectural Concern

Model version changes are a form of architecture evolution that is unique to Claude integrations. Unlike a dependency upgrade in a deterministic library — where the changelog lists exactly what changed and the test suite verifies correctness — a model version upgrade changes the behaviour of the system in ways that are not fully predictable from any static specification. The model's response to a given prompt in the new version is probabilistically similar to its response in the old version but not identical. This makes model version management an architectural concern, not just an operational one.

The ALIAS_LATEST pin strategy is the highest-risk model management pattern. Using an alias like "claude-sonnet-4" means the model the integration calls can change at any time when Anthropic updates what that alias points to, without any change in the integration's code. For integrations where response quality is business-critical, this is equivalent to allowing an unreviewed dependency upgrade to deploy silently to production. The ModelVersionPolicy structure makes the pin strategy an explicit, documented decision rather than an implicit default — teams using ALIAS_LATEST should have a specific justification for that choice.
Architecture Decision Records for AI System Boundaries

Evolutionary architecture requires documenting the decisions that shape the system's structure over time. Without this documentation, the architectural choices made at launch become invisible constraints that later engineers work around rather than revisit. For Claude integrations, the most important decisions to document are the system boundary decisions: what is handled in the prompt versus in code, what context is included versus excluded from the Claude conversation, and what the integration does when the model refuses or produces an unexpected output.

The model_upgrade_sensitivity field is specific to Claude integration ADRs. It answers the question: if Anthropic releases a new model version, how likely is this architectural decision to need revisiting? A decision to use tool use for structured data extraction is highly sensitive to model upgrade — newer models may handle structured output in JSON mode without tool use, making the tool-use architecture unnecessary. A decision to include a 500-token context window for conversation history is stable across model versions. Tagging decisions with their sensitivity enables the team to quickly identify which ADRs to review after a model upgrade.
Summary

Incremental architecture evolution for Claude integrations follows a four-step pattern: Stabilise (add abstractions before changing code), Extract (move to a separate layer), Test (validate against golden set before cut-over), and Cut Over (route traffic gradually with a feature flag). Architectural fitness functions enforce invariants like "all Claude calls go through the wrapper" and "prompt assembly stays in the builder" in CI, blocking merges that violate them. The strangler fig pattern applies to large prompt architecture migrations: the StranglerFigRouter uses deterministic request-hash-based routing for user consistency, with a shadow mode that logs new-path outputs without serving them. The ParallelRunLog captures the output comparison across the full production request distribution and provides a migration_readiness gate based on divergence rate and human review queue size.
